does: This paper reframes mechanistic interpretability as a graph machine-learning problem by converting activation-patching profiles into patch-effect graphs that represent causal circuits in transformers. The authors develop three graph-construction methods—direct-influence via causal mediation, partial-correlation, and co-influence—and apply graph kernels to analyze these structures. Evaluating on GPT-2 Small using the Indirect Object Identification task and variants, they demonstrate that patch-effect graphs preserve discriminative signals about model computations, with localized edge-slot features yielding higher classification accuracy than global graph descriptors. Their validation approach using screened paired-patching confirms that selected edges from correlation and co-influence methods correspond to stronger causal effects, providing quantitative evidence that the graph representation captures meaningful circuit structure.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability literature pioneered by techniques like activation patching and causal circuit analysis. It responds to the scalability challenge identified in prior work: as patching studies expand across diverse prompts and tasks, the resulting intervention data becomes high-dimensional and difficult to compare systematically. The paper builds on foundational mechanistic studies of transformers, particularly work on the IOI task which has become a benchmark for interpretability research. By applying graph-kernel methods, it incorporates machine-learning tools for structured comparison that were previously underutilized in mechanistic analysis. The approach complements existing circuit-identification techniques by providing a principled framework for organizing and comparing intervention patterns across different model components and task instances.
enables: This framework opens new directions for systematic circuit discovery and comparison at scale. By representing mechanistic interventions as graphs, it enables querying which circuit motifs generalize across task families and which are task-specific, supporting systematic taxonomy of transformer computations. The graph-kernel approach could be extended to identify recurring circuit patterns across different model scales, architectures, or training regimes. It sets up possibilities for using graph-neural-network classifiers to predict circuit structure from raw model weights or intermediate representations, potentially accelerating circuit identification. The method also points toward automated circuit discovery pipelines that leverage graph structure to guide patching experiments more efficiently, and could facilitate cross-model comparisons to understand how different models implement similar computational tasks through different circuit topologies.
